conditions: all mandatory document types and sections contain substantive content on first readiness check; binary gate (pass/fail)
regulatory_anchor: CAP 722A application intake process
reasoning: PackCompleteness is binary by design—pack either passes acceptance gate or rejected for rework. Tolerable = 100% per Alexa's scale. Industry baseline (72%) reflects widespread rework cycles; constraint is to improve upstream intake practices.

unit: count of evidence items per aircraft
deadline: by SORA Assessment gate (before first flight trial)
conditions: at least 3 distinct SORA evidence items per aircraft addressing swarm-specific failure modes (loss-of-separation, loss-of-coordination, emergent-failure)
regulatory_anchor: JARUS SORA v2.5 swarm-aware risk taxonomy
reasoning: JARUS SORA v2.5 (2025) introduced swarm guidance. Status 15% far below Ideal 80%. Tolerable set at >= 3 items/aircraft as absolute minimum for defensible risk assessment. Below this, authorization is unattainable.

unit: count of evidence items per aircraft
conditions: JARUS SORA v2.5 conformance mandates >= 5 distinct evidence items per aircraft for complex swarms (>= 3 aircraft)
regulatory_anchor: JARUS SORA v2.5 Appendix C (swarm-specific risk assessment methodology)
reasoning: Regulatory minimum from JARUS SORA v2.5 published guidance. Industry adoption slow (15%) due to novelty.

conditions: ConOps sections explicitly define nominal and worst-credible behavior for >= 85% of operational phases, with inter-rater clarity (kappa >= 0.8)
regulatory_anchor: CAP 722B ConOps clarity and ODD definition
reasoning: Status 55% significantly below Tolerable 85%. Clarity essential for crew training and safety case coherence. Current gap (30 points) indicates lack of explicit worst-credible boundaries.

conditions: Monte Carlo simulation (>= 10,000 trials) shows >= 99.5% success preventing boundary exceedance across ODD and single/dual failures
regulatory_anchor: CAP 722B loss-of-control containment criteria
reasoning: Status 99.6% already above Tolerable. Minimal gap reflects technology maturity. Swarm ops increase breach probability combinatorially.

conditions: below 99% containment, regulator cannot authorize due to excessive fly-away risk
regulatory_anchor: CAP 722B authorization revocation threshold
reasoning: Survival threshold: below 99%, uncontrolled breach risk is unacceptable and authorization refused.

conditions: for populated trial areas (residential density > 0.1/hectare), regulator demands 99.9% standard
regulatory_anchor: CAP 722B populated-area risk acceptance
reasoning: Regulatory override for populated airspace. Higher population density requires lower breach tolerance.

conditions: C2 link maintains >= 99% availability; loss triggers autonomous safe state within 30 seconds
gap_from_status: availability metric (99% vs status 45-second survival time not directly comparable)
regulatory_anchor: CAP 722B link resilience and autonomous safety
reasoning: 99% availability is binding constraint. Below this, crew cannot reliably regain control. Status (45-second survival) meets Survival (30s) but link uptime is regulator requirement.

deadline: mandatory minimum for all BVLOS operations
conditions: after C2 loss, aircraft maintains safe state (within geofence) >= 30 seconds for crew RTL trigger
regulatory_anchor: EUROCAE WG6 autonomy survivability baseline
reasoning: 30 seconds = minimum human reaction time for crew detection and RTL command. Status already meets this.

conditions: DAA resolves >= 99.5% of cooperative and non-cooperative encounter scenarios predictably, with recovery within target time and no secondary conflicts
regulatory_anchor: DO-365 MOPS + swarm coordination margin
reasoning: DO-365 baseline 99.9% for cooperative. Tolerable 99.5% accounts for non-cooperative difficulty and swarm complexity. Status 88% far below; primary gap is non-cooperative resolution.

conditions: DO-365 minimum: 99.9% detection confidence for cooperative DAA systems
regulatory_anchor: DO-365 Minimum Operational Performance Standard
reasoning: Table-stakes regulatory compliance. Non-cooperative detection is primary challenge.

unit: percentage of test flight hours within documented bounds
conditions: swarm operates within all emergent-behavior boundaries >= 95% of accumulated test flight hours; out-of-bounds events logged
regulatory_anchor: EUROCAE WG6 swarm assurance (draft 2025); RA 2330 military precedent
reasoning: Tolerable: >= 95% compliance. Status (3 behaviors identified, ~40% estimated compliance) far below. Once boundaries defined, swarm must respect them or behavior is uncontrolled.

unit: percentage of test flight hours within bounds
conditions: out-of-bounds in > 10% of hours triggers trial pause and emergent-behavior investigation
regulatory_anchor: RA 2330 behavior compliance margin precedent
reasoning: Survival: 90% compliance minimum. Higher out-of-bounds frequency suggests behaviors not well-understood. Trial pauses.

unit: percentage of decisions reconstructable by independent reviewer
conditions: Decision trees 100%; SVMs feature importance + boundary; neural networks saliency + LIME/SHAP >= 80% coverage. >= 80% safety-critical decisions independently reconstructable.
regulatory_anchor: AAIB Accident Investigation Regulations 2015 investigability requirement
reasoning: Tolerable: >= 80%. Status 28% dangerously low; 52-point gap largest in analysis. Ensures AAIB can investigate most decisions.

conditions: below 70%, AAIB refuses to investigate black-box decisions, blocking authorization
regulatory_anchor: AAIB Accident Investigation Regulations 2015
reasoning: Survival: below 70%, system is unassurable. AAIB will not investigate.

conditions: SituationalAwareness >= 2Hz nominal / >= 10Hz off-nominal; AuthorityPreservation >= 80% reversible / 100% irreversible; TakeoverLatency >= 10s; WorkloadBounds <= 3 concurrent alarms, <= 6 min active/hour. Score 70+.
regulatory_anchor: CAP 722 human oversight; EU Regulation 2019/945 operational authorization criteria
reasoning: Tolerable composite 70 reflects balance between autonomy and crew authority. Status 52 below Tolerable. Largest deficit WorkloadBounds (35/100)—swarm events overwhelm crew. Crew must retain meaningful control.

conditions: Termination triggers correctly and executes completely >= 99.9% of simulated off-nominal scenarios (>= 50 distinct). Safe configuration achieved without secondary hazards.
regulatory_anchor: CAP 722B flight termination assurance (RA 2330 military precedent)
reasoning: Tolerable: >= 99.9%. Status 99.1% nearly at Tolerable. Swarms add complexity: multi-aircraft termination must coordinate to prevent mid-air collisions during descent.

conditions: below 99%, flight ops cannot authorize due to uncontrolled descent risk
regulatory_anchor: CAP 722B authorization gate
reasoning: Survival: failures > 1% make uncontrolled descent probable. Status meets this.

unit: percentage of incident events with complete recorded evidence
conditions: All incidents logged with complete state (XYZ, velocity, accel, heading, engine, autonomy, sensors, crew, alerts) >= 10Hz, no gaps > 100ms, forensic provenance verified, retained for MOR + 1 year.
regulatory_anchor: CAA Occurrence Reporting Regulations; AAIB Accident Investigation Regulations 2015
reasoning: Tolerable = 100%: either fully logged or investigation compromised. Status 88% indicates gaps in logging and provenance adoption.

conditions: Each crew member: (1) CAA UAS operator cert, (2) aircraft type rating, (3) swarm-operations endorsement, (4) role-specific training (pass/fail), (5) emergency-procedure sim >= 90% success. Plus minimum hours (50 pilot, 20 sensor op).
regulatory_anchor: CAP 722C crew licensing and training requirements
reasoning: Tolerable: 5 competencies per crew member. Status 3.5 below. Gap reflects nascent swarm-operations endorsement adoption.

unit: percentage coverage with no gaps or overlaps
conditions: Signed CAA/MAA apportionment covers 100% SORA hazards, 100% design domains, 100% operational domains. Each aspect assigned to exactly one body. No gaps, no overlaps.
regulatory_anchor: CAP 722 CAA/MAA coordination; MOD/CAA joint governance
reasoning: Binary: responsibility either clear or ambiguous. Status 80% = 20% ambiguity. Novel domains (swarm coordination, emergent behavior, ML assurance) lack clear ownership.

unit: percentage of gates with documented exit/reversal criteria
conditions: Every phased gate has: clear criterion, documented exit/reversal procedure, data immutability (write-once or audit-log), re-entry criteria. All evidence preserved.
gap_from_status: N/A — binary design requirement (either all gates reversible or not)
regulatory_anchor: EUROCAE WG6 phased trial guidance (draft 2025); military T&E doctrine (reversible gates)
reasoning: Tolerable = all gates reversible by design. Status 2.5 gates far below Ideal >= 6. Reversibility essential: trial pauses if emergent behavior detected, rather than cancels.

conditions: Engaged >= 5 groups (residents within 5km, landowners, environmental, aviation, media) with prior notification (>= 30 days), feedback collection, documented response, escalation of residual concerns.
regulatory_anchor: CAP 1616 airspace change consultation process
reasoning: Tolerable per CAP 1616: >= 5 groups, >= 30-day lead. Status 2 far below. Social license critical for BVLOS swarms in populated areas. REQ017 has no user story but CAP 1616 applies.

conditions: For trials within 5km of residential areas, engage >= 6 groups (add local council as mandatory 6th per enhanced CAP 1616)
regulatory_anchor: CAP 1616 populated-area consultation mandate; GDPR data protection
reasoning: Regulatory override for populated trials. Higher engagement (6 groups, local council) required for planning coordination and social license.
